sentences.text: Yes.
I don't remember.
He died a couple of years ago.
That's all I know.
But my son would know, I'm sure.
Yeah.
He had me at the kommandantur and wanted to know why I, you know, being British, being English.
But he didn't-- I don't suppose he knew this either.
When you married-- before the war, when you married a foreigner, you lost your nationality.
But after the war, because there were so many English women caught, they restored it.
So if they marry out now, they get-- they keep their nationality.
Yes.
She was a SS woman.
I never saw her, but I heard about her.
I never saw her.
But the SS used to come and count us, and next to me, one side was my little daughter, as I said, and next to me was the-- the wife of a specialist, dent-- a medical specialist in Holland.
And she turned her face round one day, so this SS sergeant, who was counting us, kicked her in the stomach.
And she collapsed.
And we weren't allowed to bring her in, to take her in.
She had to lie there till they-- after we were counted, and they said we could take her in the barrack.
I don't know what happened to her after that.
I don't know.
Oh, we were-- you can imagine what we thought.
You know, you-- you've got to do everything they tell you and not open your mouth.
But we were all very downcast, very upset, as you can imagine.
Not really, no, because I still had my family with me for a time.
So, no, I wasn't frightened.
And I thought maybe being born in England might help.

But my sister's husband died in Belsen, and they kept her with another group, thinking-- that was much, much later-- thinking, after the war, they could exchange her or-- or, you know, for prisoners in England or something.
Yeah.
They kept her and my nephew.
No, she was a-- she was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Americans came and--
--liberated her.
But by that time, she'd gone with the other women that were with her in a train to somewhere else.
They said, let's get the train.
Let's go somewhere else.
So I don't know the exact place where the Americans got her, freed her.
Yes.
No, nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
We never had a number or anything there.
Our group, anyway.
They used to shout [INAUDIBLE].
But never-- that's all we knew.
And we had to get out.
It was about 5 o'clock in the morning.
Yeah.
And in the freezing cold as well.
Oh, yeah.
Yes, everybody in the barracks except the very-- if there were any babies.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We-- we, at that time, still had our own clothes.
Yeah.
I was in Belsen-- one night-- one December night, they called the men to the kommandantur.
And they wanted to know the names and addresses of any diamond people in Amsterdam, Jewish or not Jewish.
And they-- of course they didn't know where the Jewish were.
They were probably underground, you know, in hiding.
But they knew all the others.
My husband-- I think Maurice told you where.
I think my son told you where my husband went?
Ah, yeah.
I went to a-- they called the men up at-- I must tell you this.
They callled the men up on the 4th of December, and they took him away-- we couldn't say good-bye or anything-- at the same day-- date-- same date-- same time that my mother died in London.
I didn't hear that till after the war.
Then, the next day, they called the children and the women out, separated with the case-- our case-- we still had our suitcases.
So they said the children go to the left with the suitcases, and the women go to the right.
Right?
So we stood there.
And then they said, you women are walking outside the barracks.
And the transport will be there.
And the children will come on the trucks that are taking the luggage as well.
That's the last time I saw my children, [SOBBING] because they didn't bring the children.
They were all trucks there for us to go in, and we wouldn't go.
So we were beaten into the-- into the trucks.
[CRYING]
[SOBBING] Then, we went on transport in those trucks to the proper concentration camp.
That was a real concentration camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, when we were in the trucks.
When we had to-- when they took us away, we realized.
That's when they beat us into the truck, because we realized the children weren't coming.
We waited and waited as long as they wanted us to.
That's when we realized it.
So you can imagine how we all felt.
There was 168 of us.
I don't really remember how many children in all.
What did you think is going to happen to the children?
Didn't know.
Didn't realize what was-- scared.
We were-- we still didn't know about the Auschwitz and Birkenau.
We didn't know, because they told them that they were going to a working camp.
They didn't know better, either.
Pardon?
Yes, a big camp.
And German women-- either prostitutes or murderesses-- they were all taken out of prisons, and they were over us.
And they said, you're free to do whatever you like.
But they did nothing to us.
They used to get us up in the morning, because we had to go and work in the salt mine.
So the SS used to come 5:00 in-- they took-- first of all, when we first got there, we were all mixed.
All mixed.
And there was a Belgian woman.
She must've been anti-Fascist.
She was there, but she was free, and she was walking up and down.
There were other groups in front of us.
She said, stay behind.
And she said, if you've got anything you want to save, give it to me, and I promise you'll get it back.
Well, we still had our wedding rings, five of us in a row.
That was always what it was.
So five of us, another English girl married to a cousin of my husband and three other friends from Amsterdam, so we took our wedding rings off.
And I had a wallet that-- with my mother's Red Cross letter in it.
So I gave her that, all of that-- the wallet and the Red Cross letter-- and I gave it to her.
And I thought, well, it would have gone in any case.
So we've got a chance.
But she was good, because my son still has the wallet.
Non-Jewish, yeah.
But anti-Fascist.
But they didn't know that.
Do I remember them?
I remember them, but I don't know their names.
Only the English girl that was married to a cousin of my husband.
Her name was Sarah.
Sarah.
And also Rabbie.
Yeah.
Well, she walked up and down, so very softly, very quietly, very-- yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
She spoke Belgian, which I understood, being Dutch.
And the others did, too.
So-- but very softly.
We managed to give it to her.
So, I just want to tell you this.
When we got-- when it was our turn to come forward to the SS-- the others had already gone-- they stripped us of everything.
And all we got was like a long chemise.
No pants, no bra, no nothing.
And we knotted it in between our legs.
For-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then we went to the barracks.
It was a great big room.
No windows, no nothing.
Two on a bed.
Two on a bunk.
Yeah.
And they used to get us up at 5 o'clock in the morning.
No hygiene.
[LAUGHS] We never had a-- a bit of soap or a towel or anything.
No hygiene at all.
Nothing.
They used-- let me tell you how-- what happened.
Then in the morning, they used to get us up at 5 o'clock in the morning.
And with the SS, five in a row, they marched us from the barrack to a-- to a mine.
It was a salt mine.
And we went down in the whatever they call it-- into the salt mine.
When we got the salt mine, it was-- salt mine.
They took us to a big room, and there was all the Hitler and all that stuff-- flags and everything-- all around on the walls.
And big machines, big machines.
Electric machines.
And we noticed that a couple of the machines that we were in front of came from Russia.
There was a-- it must've been a manager sitting at a desk, and they put us one behind the other on these machines.
The SS women with the dogs were still with us-- not the men.
And just interrupting that walking to the mine shaft, they used to sing all their songs.
So-- and it was dark at 5 o'clock in the morning.
So we used to try and remember the name of the stars that you learned at school just to keep our sanity for the children.
You know, that we didn't know where they were.
So when we got down there, we were put behind these electric machines thinking, I suppose, being in this group, we knew about electric machines.
And they were making munitions there.
Well, they put us behind, and we had to start the-- whatever we made wasn't right, so we were scared.
But before us, there was a group of men-- not Jewish-- brought in to work from different countries.
And they made these things.
So when they realized that we couldn't do it, they left good ones behind.
And put the-- and next to us was a cabinet with all shelves with, I suppose, the materials in them.
So we used to put what we did wrong under there.
But the woman gave me the wallet back, and one of us used to take care of it every day and put it under there.
And then we thought it was getting too risky, so we didn't have toilets in the camp itself.
We had latrines.
We used to dig it in the earth under the latrine, and that's why my son has it now, because the day we were going-- evacuated from there, I picked it up and took it with us.
Yes.
There was no hygiene.
When we-- at 12 o'clock, they used to-- the SS women used to take us to the toilets.
There were toilets there.
So we used to take the water-- try and take the water out of the cistern on top.
It was old-fashioned.
You had to pull a chain and get the water, and we used to wipe our faces.
Some of us tried to drink the water.
But we had nothing to drink all the time we were down there.
What did the women--
12 Hours we were down there every day.
We didn't.
The strangest thing was when we were already in a-- in a Belsen, before we went, they stopped completely, which was a good thing for us.
Stopped completely, completely.
Never thought about it at all.
Relieved, but that was all.
Yes.
No, only our group.
We-- only--  only our group of women worked in that particular place.
The men were digging the salt in a different area.
But we used to see them in passing going to the shaft-- you know, to go up.
And they used to-- one of them or the other would whisper something.
And at a certain time, they whispered, they're loosing.
We didn't know whether to trust them or believe them.
We never spoke to them, because we were afraid, you know.
Then one morning-- and when we came back from the shaft, I will tell you, they gave us two pieces, two small pieces, of ersatz bread and a cup of ersatz coffee.
That was our ration for the day, because we worked down there.
Then one morning, we get back to the mine, and all of a sudden, we had to go back to the camp.
We were told we're being evacuated from the camp.
So we were back in the trucks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we had to go to the bahnhof-- to get brought to the bahnhof to get to the truck.
What is the bahnhof?
A railway station.
That's German.
That's German for 'railway station.' So we got in the trucks again.
And the SS were still with us.
And we couldn't sit because by that time, there was all nationalities-- Polish girls and girls from other towns in Europe.
So we stood.
So they used to stop in the mornings, and we had to climb out.
And they-- somebody next to you was dead and you didn't know, so you had to take them out and be counted as well.
Then we-- by that time we were getting a bit weak.
But, you know, we helped each other.
Then all of a sudden, we're in the trucks, and they-- we were going between the railway lines from one place to another.
They were shunting us.
And all of a sudden, there must've been a dog-- a fight up there, and we had to get out of the trucks and the SS took us to a field.
And we all had to get down.
But they had to get down.
We thought we were going to be shot.
But they had to get down as well.
Then, they took us back in.
And we were back and forth between the lines.
I don't know how far we went.
But one of the girls thought through a crack she saw Lubeck, but I'm not sure about that.
Anyway, [SIGH] then one morning we were counted, and the kommand-- the kommandant, whoever he was, said, if I don't get you into the camp tonight, that'll-- that's-- that'll be it.
Well--
I don't know.
I've got no idea what it was.
Don't know.
Just saw the name-- this girl just saw the name.
So we went over the River Elbe in Germany.
And it was blown up after we'd left.
And they took us to a camp in Hamburg.
When we got there, we were all mixed up-- stars and no-stars, and-- Means Jews and non-Jews?
Jewish and non-Jewish.
And the kommandant came out and he says, you-- you lot-- just like that-- go to the right.
The others can all go into the barracks.
So we stood there.
He says, you can stay there till you drop.
So we were lucky that day. 12 o'clock that night, the unterscharfuhrer comes out, and he says, you can go into the barrack.
Two on a bunk, which we were thankful we did.
We were there for eight days.
They fed us, like, a raw potato-- no, boiled potato, beetroot, all that kind of thing.
A bowl a day.
No, I don't.
We didn't know his name, so I couldn't remember.
Then one morning, he gets us out, and he said, you're all going on transport today.
So we-- one of the girls spoke German.
We weren't allowed to speak to them before, . but she spoke German, and she asked him where we were going.
So he said, you're going to Denmark.
So we said, well, that's no good.
Denmark's also occupied.
So the next morning we had to queue up.
And there was one of our girls, she was a shirt maker.
So she used to-- they used-- one-- an old soldier, not an SS, an old soldier used to pick her up and take her to where the SS.
She used to have to make shirts for them.
So when we get to the bahnhof where the trucks were, she spoke to him.
And she said our group would like to stay.
All right?
We'd like to stay, she said, together.
So he said, well, wait till the end.
He said, and what's more, there's going to be straw on the floor.
Anyway, that's what happened.
We waited till the end, and we were in this truck where there was straw on the floor.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we get to Den-- to Padborg.
That's the first crossing in Denmark.
The SS were with us.
They opened the doors, and then we saw Danish people throwing parcels over the big wire fence.
So the SS went and picked them out-- up, because they didn't have any food, either.
They went and picked them up, and they were gone.
The SS were gone.
They shut the trucks again, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were off.
When they stopped eventually, we were in Copenhagen.
And there was all Red Cross on the station with food.
But we couldn't stomach it.
We couldn't.
Then from there, they put us in coaches and sent us to a cleansing station.
They took what we had on off.
They-- they shaved our heads.
They gave us a clean towel and a piece of soap.
There were doctors and nurses there and showers.
And that was the best present I'd had in my life-- a clean towel and a piece of soap.
When we got in the shower, we couldn't stand, some of us.
We sat, and we didn't want to get out.
But we got out, and the nurses dried us and helped us and gave us clothes-- shoes and under clothes and dress.
And then they took us to a big school that had been converted, and there were all mattresses on the floor.
And we slept there overnight.
The next morning, they got us up and took us to the seaport in Copenhagen.
And all Red Cross were there.
They put us on a boat, and on the boat we had big forms to fill in-- where we were born, where were taken, everything, where we had been.
And because I was born in England, they-- but married in Holland, and the other English girl-- they put us with the Dutch group, which was natural, I suppose.
Right.
Then we got to the harbor in Malmo, Sweden.
And the first-- and they said, this is where you're going to say.
So-- and we were all separated then, the Dutch and the French and whoever other nationality.
We were separated then.
And the first to leave were the French.
And the people from the French embassy came, and they sang And when they saw the state they were in, they were all crying.
You can imagine.
